doc:
Seven Years' War:
 
 American Revolutionary War:
 
 Admiral comte Pierre André de <tgr>Suffren</tgr> de Saint Tropez, bailli de <tgr>Suffren</tgr>  , Château de Saint-Cannat) was a French Navy officer and admiral. Beginning his career during the War of the Austrian Succession, he fought in the Seven Years' War, where he was taken prisoner at the Battle of Lagos. Promoted to captain in 1772, he was one of the aids of Admiral d'Estaing during the Naval battles of the American Revolutionary War, notably taking part in the Siege of Savannah. <tgr>Suffren</tgr> was then appointed to serve in the Indian Ocean under Thomas d'Estienne d'Orves, but assumed command himself at his death. Leading a 15-ship squadron, he fought five intense and evenly matched battles for control of the sea against Vice-Admiral Sir Edward Hughes. Through these battles, <tgr>Suffren</tgr> managed to secure French dominance of the seas in Indian waters until the conclusion of the war at the Treaty of Paris. At his return, he was promoted to vice-admiral. He died shortly before he was to take command of the Brest squadron of the French fleet. Pierre André de <tgr>Suffren</tgr> was born on 17 July 1729 in the Château de Saint-Cannat to the family of Marquis Paul de <tgr>Suffren</tgr>, the third son of an old nobility from Provence with two daughters and three other sons. In October 1743, as the War of the Austrian Succession was raging, <tgr>Suffren</tgr>, aged 14, went to Toulon to undertaken naval studies as a Garde-Marine. However, he spent only 6 months ashore before he was appointed on a ship. <tgr>Suffren</tgr> served on the 64-gun Solide and took part in the Battle of Toulon in 1744. During the battle, Solide engaged HMS Northumberland. In the spring of 1745, <tgr>Suffren</tgr> transferred on Pauline, part of a 5-ship and 2-frigate squadron under Captain Jean-Baptiste Mac Nemara, sent to America to harasse British forces.
pred_ans:
| Admiral | Pierre André de Suffren de Saint Tropez |

| Wars | Seven Years' War, American Revolutionary War |
| Notable Battles | Siege of Savannah, battles against Vice-Admiral Sir Edward Hughes |
| Promotions | Captain in 1772, Vice-Admiral post-war |
| Notable Achievements | Secured French dominance in Indian waters |
| Death | Shortly before taking command of the Brest squadron |

doc: SS <tgr>Ryusei Maru</tgr>  was a Japanese transport ship that was sunk by USS Rasher on 25 February 1944. Nearly 5,000 lives were lost. This article about a specific military ship or boat of Japan is a stub. You can help Wikipedia by expanding it. This World War II article is a stub. You can help Wikipedia by expanding it.
pred_ans:

doc: <tgr>Sebastiano Venier</tgr>   was Doge of Venice from 11 June 1577 to 3 March 1578. He is best remembered in his role as the Venetian admiral at the Battle of Lepanto. Venier was born in Venice around 1496. He was a son of Moisè  Venier and Elena Donà, and a nephew of Zuan Francesco Venier, Co-Lord of Cerigo. He was a paternal grandson of Moisé Venier . He was the great-great-great-grandson of Pietro Venier, Governor of Cerigo. He worked as a lawyer from a very early age, though without holding formal qualifications, and subsequently was an administrator for the government of the Republic of Venice. In 1570 he was procurator of St Mark's and, in the December of the same year, capitano generale da Mar of the Venetian fleet in the new war against the Ottoman Turks, substituting Girolamo Zane. He was the commander of the Venetian contingent at Battle of Lepanto , in which the Christian League decisively defeated the Turks. After the peace he returned to Venice as a very popular figure, and in 1577, at the age of 81, he was unanimously elected Doge. He was married to Cecilia Contarini, who bore him a daughter, Elena Venier. He also had two illegitimate sons, Filippo and Marco. Venier later provided Filippo with a benefice and Marco, who became a functionary of the chancellery, with a post in Dalmatia. <tgr>Sebastiano Venier</tgr> died on 3 March 1578. He was interred in the Santa Maria degli Angeli of Murano, instead of the family's tomb, as he had expressed in 1568 in his will. However, in 1907 his remains were moved in the Basilica di San Giovanni e Paolo, a traditional burial place of the doges.

doc: <tgr>Tembien</tgr> is a historic region in Tigray Region and former provinces of Ethiopia. It is a mountainous area of that country. During the reforms in 1994–95, the old provinces were replaced with regions, zones and woredas. The area of the former province is now split over the woredas of Dogua <tgr>Tembien</tgr> and Kola <tgr>Tembien</tgr>. It was located east of the Semien Province and north of Abergele, a historic district of the Begemder province. On the east, it was bordered by the Enderta Province. The original capital of the province was Melfa, west of the current town of Hagere Selam; later on Abiy Addi, nowadays located in Kola <tgr>Tembien</tgr> , became the capital. The region reached a highpoint in the Tsatsen mountains at 2828 meters above sea level, just south of Hagere Selam. <tgr>Tembien</tgr> holds numerous prehistoric sites, which have been dated to the Middle Stone Age in Ayninbirkekin, or Pastoral Neolithic in Aregen and Menachek. The Dabo Zellelew cave in Aregen at a height of about 2000 metres, has been explored over 14.4 m but its distance is claimed to be way longer . It contains lithic tools, potsherds, engravings and paintings of Pastoral Neolithic age. The Mihdar Ab’ur cave in the village of Mahba in Aregen at a height of about 2500 metres, is some 64 m long . It contains engravings and paintings of Pastoral Neolithic age. The Danei Kawlos cave in the Tsech'i gorge at the west of  Menachek at a height of about 2020 metres, is some 13.5 metres long . It contains lithic tools, potsherds, and faunal remains of Pastoral Neolithic age. Further down, the May Ila open-air site in the Tsech'i gorge at the extreme west of Menachek at a height of about 1990 metres . It contains blades, blade cores, and a few potsherds of Pastoral Neolithic age.
